original_abstract:
The policing of rape is currently in a state of permacrisis. This book investigates why police investigations consistently fail the majority of rape victim-survivors and proposes a rigorous new framework designed to fix this broken system. The "rape justice gap" is currently defined by abysmally low conviction rates and investigative processes that many survivors find re-traumatizing. While thirty years of legal and policy tweaks have attempted to address these issues, the reality for most survivors remains grueling.

Police institutions are notoriously resistant to reform, even as public trust continues to plummet. This book argues that meaningful change requires a fundamental shift in perspective: rather than asking why rape is so difficult to investigate, we must ask what it is about the police as an institution that makes investigating rape so difficult. 

*Policing Rape* introduces a practice-oriented theoretical framework intended to radically and sustainably transform investigations, prevention strategies, and the quality of officer engagement. The authors provide a detailed reflection on putting this theory into practice through Operation Soteria Bluestone—a major academic-police collaboration that serves as a blueprint for a national transformation of rape policing. This book is essential reading for anyone committed to understanding and closing the justice gap for survivors of sexual violence.
humanized_abstract:
New Studies in Ethnic Politics POLICING RAPE How to investigate sexual assault without falling apart First publication, Oxford University Press, 2024 The policing of rape is in a state of permanent crisis. Fixing Rape Investigations is the first book to take on the significant challenge of understanding what has to change, in institutions and practices, in order to fix the system, and to introduce an innovative methodology and resulting framework through which policing rape can be transformed, and more survivors can achieve justice through the state. Officially we are still thirty years into a 'revolution in rape proceedings' that aims to generate greater rape justice to the benefit of victim-survivors. The level of the rape justice gap—comprised of low rape convictions and traumatic investigations for victim-survivors—remains unacceptably dire. The many attempts to address these problems via legislative and regulatory reform over the past three decades have apparently had little effect on the experience of most victims.

Rape investigations in Sweden, as in other countries, are a pressing problem that appears to be impenetrable to reform. In fact, it is hard to think of an institution in society where there has been fewer attempts at reform during recent years, not least in a time when trust in the police is at a record low. Instead of focusing on the question of why rape investigations are so problematic, the aim in this book is to turn the question on its head and examine the properties of a police institution that may make the business of investigating sexual crimes as complicated as it is.

Policing Rape offers a practice-oriented theoretical framework aimed at transforming rape investigations and prevention work, and improving the way police officers interact with victims of sexual violence. The authors offer a reflexive account of their involvement in Operation Soteria Bluestone, a pioneering academic-police partnership that aims to improve the way the police handle sexual violence complaints. This book is a must read for anyone concerned to reduce the justice gap for survivors of rape and sexual violence.
